Article.ArticleTitle: Adding Yoga to Physical Activity Guidelines Reduces Mortality Risk Compared With Meeting Guidelines Alone.

Abstract.AbstractText: This study examined associations of yoga participation and prospective all-cause mortality risk in the United States' nationally representative National Health Interview Survey.

Data were from the National Health Interview Survey samples from 2007, 2012, and 2017. A subset of participants were asked about their weekly physical activity (PA; min/wk) and if they practiced yoga in the past year (yes/no). Participants were ≥18 years, had eligible mortality follow-up, and answered PA and yoga questions (N = 26,055). Participants were followed up using the National Death Index (a resource that provides mortality data from death certificates) through 2019 to determine participant mortality status (n = 815 deaths). Participant activity level was categorized as meeting (active) or not meeting (inactive) combined aerobic and strength training PA guidelines (≥150 min/wk of moderate-to-vigorous PA and ≥2 d/wk of strength training) based on their reported activity. Participants were categorized into 4 groups by their yoga participation and meeting combined PA guidelines (inactive/no yoga, inactive/yoga, active/no yoga, and active/yoga). Survey weighted Cox proportional hazard models computed hazard ratios mortality during follow-up between the 4 groups with active/no yoga as the reference.

In the fully adjusted models, the active/yoga group had 51% lower risk of mortality compared with active/no yoga (hazard ratio: 0.49; 95% CI, 0.26-0.91), whereas the inactive/no yoga group had 77% higher risk compared with active/no yoga (hazard ratio: 1.77; 95% CI, 1.12-2.80).
Yoga participation and meeting the combined PA guidelines may provide additional and independent protective mortality benefits compared with meeting PA guidelines without yoga.

Department of Epidemiology and Biostatistics, West Virginia University, Morgantown, WV, USA.
